text:
In this reflective retrospective Robin D.G. Kelley turns his mind to his early years as a scholar of the South African liberation movement, by recalling his 1986 *Ufahamu* article on the Comintern and the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA). Thirty years on, Kelley revisits the ‘Native Republic’ thesis of the 1928 Comintern directive to the CPSA, and reflects on its implications for our understanding of the anti-imperialist history of South Africa. Kelley’s argument is that the “Native Republic” was not something imposed from the outside, but was rather a policy that was radicalized from below by African and Coloured nationalists in the CPSA, and which transformed a European-led communist party into a party that in its practices, ideology and goals was at the forefront of Black consciousness and the Black land struggles in South Africa.

This essay explores the connection between the intellectual context of anti-apartheid activism at UCLA in the 1980s and the present political situation in post-1994 South Africa. Kelley explores the paradox of the South African Communist Party's (SACP) continuing alliance with an African National Congress (ANC) government that has abandoned radical socioeconomic goals in favor of accommodation with neoliberal economic structures. Kelley examines the SACP's critique of the market-friendly ANC and the surge of the populist, left-wing Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) as a measure of the ongoing relevance of the ancient radical vision of building a workers' and peasants' republic. Drawing on decades of engagement and reflection, Kelley combines personal intellectual history with an effort to understand the current stalemate in the long-standing struggle for a socialist South Africa, and to assess the prospects for building an economy that truly serves the needs of all people.
